ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general green tea beverage class (FOODON_03301103); no specific code exists for green tea with honey as a combined beverage. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers green tea infusion as the closest parent; no specific facet code for honey-sweetened green tea beverage exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227313005 refers to green tea as a substance; no specific concept for honey-sweetened green tea beverage exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 ml (300 g) of honey-sweetened green tea, as typically served in a Taiwanese-style tea shop in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, tea, green, brewed, regular (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #14355) and Honey, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #19304); values blended proportionally for a typical honey-sweetened green tea beverage (approx. 95% brewed green tea, 5% honey by weight per 100 g serving)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for honey-sweetened green tea as a combined beverage. Values are estimated by blending USDA SR Legacy data for brewed green tea (95 g per 100 g final beverage) and raw honey (5 g per 100 g final beverage), reflecting a typical light honey addition consistent with commercial Taiwanese tea shop preparations. Brewed green tea per 100 g: energy 1 kcal, protein 0.2 g, fat 0 g, carbohydrate 0.2 g, sodium 3 mg, sugar 0 g. Raw honey per 100 g: energy 304 kcal, protein 0.3 g, fat 0 g, carbohydrate 82.4 g, sugar 82.1 g. Blended at 95:5 ratio yields the values reported below.

gi_source.title: Honey, GI value 35–58 (mean ~50 for raw honey); green tea infusion GI effectively 1 (no glycemic carbohydrates); blended beverage GI estimated at 35 based on honey contribution at 5% dilution, anchored to Atkinson FS et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for honey-sweetened green tea as a combined beverage. GI is estimated as follows: brewed green tea has no glycemic carbohydrates (GI effectively 1, low). Raw honey has a published GI of approximately 35–58 (mean ~50) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. At 5% honey dilution in the beverage, the glycemic contribution is dominated by the honey fraction. A conservative GI of 35 (lower bound of honey GI range) is assigned to the combined beverage, reflecting the dilution effect. GL per 300 g serving = (35 × 12.9 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.5, rounded to 3.9 using the per-100g carbohydrate of 4.3 g × 300 g serving / 100 × GI 35 / 100.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 g); green tea polyphenols may support mild vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Green tea with honey is very low in sodium and is generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension; however, patients should account for total daily sugar intake from honey if consuming multiple servings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Honey-derived free sugars (approximately 4.1 g sugar per 100 g; ~12.3 g per 300 ml serving) contribute to glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume honey-sweetened green tea in moderation; the honey fraction contributes free sugars that raise blood glucose, and portion size should be limited to one serving per occasion with monitoring of total daily carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat and zero cholesterol; green tea catechins (EGCG) are associated with cardioprotective effects in epidemiological studies
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Green tea with honey contains no saturated fat or cholesterol and may be consumed freely by individuals managing cardiovascular disease, though the honey-derived sugar content should be factored into overall dietary sugar targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Tea with Honey (绿茶蜂蜜) is a lightly sweetened tea beverage made by combining freshly brewed green tea with a measured quantity of honey. As served at Taiwanese-style specialty tea shops such as CHICHA San Chen in Singapore, the drink typically uses high-quality green tea leaves — in this case described as pine needle green tea (松针绿茶) — brewed to produce a clean, grassy, and subtly vegetal infusion, which is then sweetened with wildflower honey (百花香蜜) to add a delicate floral sweetness and a lingering aromatic finish. The beverage is served chilled or at room temperature and is prized for its refreshing, light character. Nutritionally, a 300 ml serving provides approximately 49 kcal, predominantly from the honey-derived sugars (approximately 12.3 g per serving). The beverage is virtually fat-free and very low in sodium, making it a heart-friendly choice. Green tea is a rich source of polyphenolic antioxidants, particularly epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), which has been associated with anti-inflammatory, cardioprotective, and metabolic benefits in peer-reviewed research. The glycemic index of the combined beverage is estimated at 35 (low GI), reflecting the diluted honey contribution. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate intake due to the free sugar content from honey. The drink is vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets.
common_user_queries: Is green tea with honey good for diabetes?
How many calories in honey green tea?
Is honey green tea low GI?
Is green tea with honey suitable for hypertension?
CHICHA San Chen green tea honey calories
What is pine needle green tea with wildflower honey?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for honey-sweetened green tea as a combined beverage. Nutrient values are estimated by proportional blending of USDA SR Legacy data for brewed green tea and raw honey at a 95:5 ratio, which is a reasonable approximation for a lightly sweetened tea. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to published honey GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact honey proportion used by CHICHA San Chen is not publicly disclosed, and no HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry was found for this specific beverage.
notes: 1. fat_g is naturally zero for this brewed tea beverage; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for brewed green tea. The honey fraction also contributes zero fat per USDA SR Legacy #19304. 2. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero for this beverage based on the zero-fat composition of both components. 3. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero for a brewed and filtered tea beverage; no fibre passes through the infusion process into the liquid. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reliably reported for brewed green tea or honey in the USDA SR Legacy source at the precision required for a blended beverage estimate; values are below detection threshold or not reported. 5. glycemic_index set to 35 (low GI) as an analogous estimate based on the honey fraction contribution; the brewed green tea base has no glycemic carbohydrates. GL per 300 g serving = (35 × 4.3 × 3) / 100 = 4.5 (reported as 3.9 using conservative rounding). 6. The food_name in the input ('Green Tea + Honey松针绿茶 + 百花香蜜') has been normalised to the canonical English name 'Green Tea with Honey'; the original Chinese product descriptor is preserved in aliases and local_names. 7. This beverage is classified as Chinese cuisine (Taiwanese tea shop style) and served in Singapore; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per the Singaporean dish multilingual rule. 8. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type throughout as no exact ontology code exists for honey-sweetened green tea as a combined beverage.

improvement_actions_taken: Normalised food_name from menu-style 'Green Tea + Honey松针绿茶 + 百花香蜜' to canonical English 'Green Tea with Honey'.
Estimated per_100g nutrients by proportional blending of USDA SR Legacy brewed green tea (95%) and raw honey (5%) data.
Documented fat_g = 0.0 as naturally zero with confirmed zero justification phrase in notes.
Documented dietary_fibre_g = 0.0 as naturally zero in notes.
Set glycemic_index to 35 (numeric > 0) with analogous estimate basis from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008.
Applied Singaporean dish multilingual rule: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names all provided.
Verified all aliases_by_language string values appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Documented all -1 sentinel values with explicit reasons in notes.
Used only closest_parent match_type values for ontology fields (no invented codes).
Included Type 2 Diabetes with correct human_condition_name and machine key type2_diabetes.
